perturbation.original_text: 12. This Agreement is a full recourse agreement concluded under the laws of Pennsylvania and said forum shall be applicable law covering the construction, interpretation, execution, validity, enforceability, performance, and any other such matters in respect to this Agreement, including any breach or claim of breach hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. This Agreement is a full recourse agreement. The laws of another state selected by mutual agreement of involved parties will be applicable covering the construction, interpretation, execution, validity, enforceability, performance, and any other such matters in respect to this Agreement, including any breach or claim of breach hereof.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the designation of Pennsylvania law as governing the contract. Pennsylvania has specific laws protecting parties in contract disputes and requiring certain disclosures. Removing the guarantee of Pennsylvania law, even if replaced with another state's law, introduces risk because it allows for the possibility of selecting a jurisdiction with less consumer protection, or a state that may be unfavorable to one of the parties involved, especially in areas of fraud or misrepresentation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law 13 Pa.C.S. § 2201 states unless otherwise agreed the law of Pennsylvania governs. By stating that any mutually selected state law governs rather than PA law, the contract potentially contradicts the statute unless there is explicit agreement. This omission can affect contract interpretation and enforcement, particularly regarding remedies for breach of contract, potentially undermining protections afforded under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Pennsylvania law, while the changed contract allows for the selection of another state's law. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this introduces risk because the selected state may have less consumer protection. The Law Explanation says PA law governs unless otherwise agreed, implying that the change allows parties to bypass PA law. The absence of scraped law snippets does not negate the legal issue as the change allows for the potential circumvention of Pennsylvania law, creating a contradiction through omission of a guaranteed legal protection. This makes the omission impactful.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by law and construed to be in accordance with the laws of the State of Pennsylvania applicable to contracts made and to be performed solely in such State by parties thereof. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be adjudicated in arbitration under the rules of the American Arbitration Association. The prevailing party in any dispute shall be reimbursed reasonable attorneys fees.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by law and construed to be in accordance with the laws of the State of Pennsylvania applicable to contracts made and to be performed solely in such State by parties thereof. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be adjudicated in arbitration under the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the sentence 'The prevailing party in any dispute shall be reimbursed reasonable attorneys fees,' the contract now contradicts Pennsylvania's default rule on attorney's fees.  Pennsylvania follows the American Rule, which generally requires each party to bear its own attorneys' fees unless a statute or contract provides otherwise. Omitting the fee reimbursement clause eliminates a consumer's potential right to recover these fees, creating an imbalance of power during dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania Statute 42 Pa.C.S. § 2503 outlines the circumstances where counsel fees are recoverable as part of the taxable costs. While it doesn't mandate attorney fee shifting in all cases, the omission of a clause providing for it removes a potential right the parties could have agreed upon, and thus can lead to unfair outcomes that PA courts may prevent with the statute as a guideline for what constitutes reasonable costs. The removal of this clause shifts the financial burden of legal disputes entirely to the individual, which may deter legitimate claims and defenses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 2503 (2022) - Right of participants to receive counsel fees :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 25 - Representation of Litigants Section 2503 - Right of participants to receive counsel fees Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 2503 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 2503. Right of participants to receive counsel fees. The following participants shall be entitled to a reasonable counsel fee as part of the taxable costs of the matter: (1) The holder of bonds of a private corporation who successfully recovers due and unpaid interest, the liability for the payment of which was denied by the corporation. (2) A garnishee who enters an appearance in a matter which is discontinued prior to answer filed. (3) A garnishee who is found to have in his possession or control no indebtedness due to or other property of the debtor except such, if any, as has been admitted by answer filed. (4) A possessor of property claimed by two or more other persons, if the possessor interpleads the rival claimants, disclaims all interest in the property and disposes of the property as the court may direct. (5) The prevailing party in an interpleader proceeding in connection with execution upon a judgment. (6) Any participant who is awarded counsel fees as a sanction against another participant for violation of any general rule which expressly prescribes the award of counsel fees as a sanction for dilatory, obdurate or vexatious conduct during the pendency of any matter. (7) Any participant who is awarded counsel fees as a sanc
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the clause regarding the reimbursement of attorney's fees to the prevailing party. This omission contradicts the potential right for fee shifting that could have been agreed upon, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 2503, outlines specific situations where counsel fees are recoverable. While it doesn't mandate fee shifting in every case, the contract's original clause provided for it, and its removal creates an imbalance, potentially deterring legitimate claims and defenses.

perturbation.original_text: d) This Agreement and each additional agreement concluded or written or verbal disclosure made between the Parties, shall be kept confidential and is not to be reproduced, communicated or distributed in any manner whatsoever except on a "need to know" basis to persons directly involved with the closing of any transaction contemplated between the Parties, or legal counsel of a Party.
perturbation.changed_text: d) This Agreement and each additional agreement concluded or written or verbal disclosure made between the Parties, shall be kept confidential and is not to be reproduced, communicated or distributed in any manner whatsoever except on a "need to know" basis to persons directly involved with the closing of any transaction contemplated between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows disclosure to legal counsel. Removing this provision creates potential legal issues. Pennsylvania Rule of Professional Conduct 1.6 allows/requires lawyers to disclose confidential client information in certain situations (e.g., to prevent a crime). By restricting the sharing of the agreement even with legal counsel, the modified text could obstruct a party's ability to obtain necessary legal advice or comply with ethical obligations, putting them at legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania Rule of Professional Conduct 1.6 governs confidentiality of information. The omission of 'or legal counsel of a Party' prevents a party from disclosing the agreement to their lawyer, potentially hindering their ability to seek legal advice and potentially forcing the lawyer to violate ethical rules, which can override confidentiality agreements. This omission may obstruct the involved party's right to counsel.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'or legal counsel of a Party' directly contradicts the Pennsylvania Rules of Professional Conduct, specifically Rule 1.6, as it hinders a party's ability to seek legal advice and potentially forces the lawyer to violate ethical rules. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly mentions Rule 1.6 as 'Confidentiality of Information', which this omission directly impacts. The original text ensures the party can seek counsel, and the changed text removes this ability, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: a) Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, represents and warrants that it shall not conduct business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner, without the express written permission (not to be unreasonably withheld) of the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available.
perturbation.changed_text: a) Each Party, for itself and its associates as defined below, represents and warrants that it shall not conduct business with any sources or contacts, or said source's or contact's associates as defined below, that are originally made known and/or available by another Party hereto, at any time or in any manner, without the express written permission of the Party who made the source(s) known and/or available.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase '(not to be unreasonably withheld)' from the clause requiring express written permission, the contract now allows a party to arbitrarily deny permission, even if doing so is detrimental or commercially unreasonable to the other party. Pennsylvania law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, meaning parties must act reasonably. This omission creates the potential for a breach of this implied covenant, as a party could use the permission clause to unfairly block legitimate business opportunities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Northwood Investors, LLC v. Nomura Asset Capital Corp., 249 A.3d 462 (Pa. Super. 2021)
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania case law, such as Northwood Investors, LLC v. Nomura Asset Capital Corp., affirms the existence of an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By removing the requirement that permission not be unreasonably withheld, the agreement permits a party to act in bad faith, potentially preventing the other party from realizing the benefits of the contract. This contradicts the implied covenant, leading to potential legal challenges based on unfair or arbitrary denial of permission.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Barnhart, D. v. Barnhart, J. :: 2021 :: Pennsylvania Superior Court Decisions :: Pennsylvania Case Law :: Pennsylvania Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Barnhart, D. v. Barnhart, J. (memorandum) Annotate this Case Download PDF J-A14029-20 NON-PRECEDENTIAL DECISION - SEE SUPERIOR COURT I.O.P. 65.37 DONNA J. BARNHART : : : : : : : : : v. JONATHAN L. BARNHART Appellant IN THE SUPERIOR COURT OF PENNSYLVANIA No. 1472 WDA 2019 Appeal from the Order Entered August 28, 2019 In the Court of Common Pleas of Cambria County Civil Division at No(s): No. 2005-3380 BEFORE: SHOGAN, J., McLAUGHLIN, J., and MUSMANNO, J. MEMORANDUM BY McLAUGHLIN, J.: FILED SEPTEMBER 24, 2020 Jonathan L. Barnhart (“Husband”) appeals pro se from the order denying his motion to vacate a divorce decree. Husband argues the trial court abused its discretion in denying his motion because his right to due process was violated due to judicial misconduct. We affirm. Donna Barnhart (“Wife”) originally filed for divorce from Husband in 2005; the couple had three minor children. A master was appointed to hear matters of both divorce and custody in 2006. In 2011, Husband filed a “Petition for Extraordinary Jurisdiction in the [Pennsylvania] Supreme Court,” which was declined. Cambria County Judge Leahey entered a divorce decree on January 11, 2012, which incorporated the Master’s Report and Recommendation. Husband filed a timely appeal to this Court, which we quashed because outstanding issues remained and the trial court had not provided a detailed explanation of its consideration of the equitable J-A14029-20 distribution factors. Barnhart v. Barnhart, 68 A.3d 354 (Pa.Super. 2013) (Table) (unpublished memorandum). Meanwhile, during the pendency of the instant case, Husband had a child with another woman and became involved in another custody case (“Second Case”). In February 2012, Husband filed a motion to recuse Judge Leahey from the Second Case. Following a hearing, Judge Leahey issued an order stating that he found Husband’s allegations against him to be
perturbation.justification: The original text includes a clause stating permission 'not to be unreasonably withheld'. This implies a duty to act in good faith and consider the other party's interests when granting or denying permission. The changed text removes this crucial phrase, giving the party complete discretion to deny permission for any reason, or no reason at all. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing that Pennsylvania law imposes on all contracts. The removal of 'not to be unreasonably withheld' allows a party to potentially prevent the other party from realizing the benefits of the contract, as explained in the Law Explanation.
